description: Compounds with documented activity at upstream complement cascade nodes (proximal to C5a generation). Grep-verified against primary sources where PMC full-text was accessible; abstract-tier where paywalled. Per CLAUDE.md Rule 4 pre-commit verification gate. ChEMBL coverage check executed per compound.
verification_status: primary-literature anchored from PubMed abstracts + Paperclip MCP full-text grep where available; one canonical seed paper (PMID 3198307 rosmarinic acid) sourced via abstract retrieval — abstract tier explicit per CLAUDE.md gate

chembl_coverage.interpretation: ChEMBL has zero curated anticomplement bioactivity for ANY compound in this list. Anticomplement activity is a hemolytic-assay readout that ChEMBL never indexed systematically. Same coverage gap as comp-013 TCM compounds, worse here because anticomplement is a niche assay class. The discipline failure mode comp-018 explicitly avoids: treating ChEMBL absence as evidence of absence.

compounds.name: Rosmarinic acid
compounds.compound_class: plant phenolic acid (caffeic acid + 3,4-dihydroxyphenyllactic acid ester)
compounds.chembl_anticomplement_data: EMPTY (ChEMBL does not curate hemolytic-assay anticomplement data)

ic50_data.assay: C3 convertase inhibition (CH50 in vitro, sheep erythrocyte sensitized + guinea pig serum)
ic50_data.qualitative: threshold 1 µM; optimal inhibitory concentration 5-10 µM (~70% inhibition); higher concentrations LESS effective (bell-shape) — load-bearing for dose extrapolation
ic50_data.primary_source: Englberger W et al., Int J Immunopharmacol 1988;10(6):729-37 (PMID 3198307, doi:10.1016/0192-0561(88)90026-4)
ic50_data.verification_status: abstract-tier — primary source 1988 paper not in PMC; multiple downstream papers (Mu 2013 PMID 24144800; Cimanga 1999 PMID 17260306; Si 2008 PMID 19031237) cite this IC50 as positive control with values reported as 137-182 µM — DISCREPANCY between Englberger original (5-10 µM) and downstream comparator-control values (137-182 µM). Likely explanation: assay-format difference (different SRBC source / serum source / endpoint definition). Both ranges flagged. For OE platform purposes the 5-10 µM optimal is the primary-source value; the 137-182 µM is the comparator-context value seen across the 2008-2013 literature.

ic50_data.assay: in vivo cobra venom factor paw oedema (rat)
in vivo CVF-induced ARDS-like lung injury (rat)
ic50_data.result: RMA blocked CVF-induced neutropenia, lung neutrophil migration, bronchoalveolar leakage, blood pressure changes, TNF-α elevation — comparable magnitude to direct C5aR/C3aR antagonists
ic50_data.primary_source: Proctor LM et al., Int Immunopharmacol 2006;6(8):1224-32 (PMID 16782534, doi:10.1016/j.intimp.2006.03.002)

ic50_data.assay: in vivo polycystic kidney disease (Pkd1-/- mouse + Han:SPRD Cy/+ rat)
ic50_data.dose_mg_kg: RMA reduced creatinine -50%, BUN -78%, kidney/body weight ratio -60%, renal cystic index -60%

compounds.bioavailability_oral: Variable — plasma half-life ~1.5-2h after oral dose; gut-first-pass extensive; aqueous-soluble; absorbed in upper GI per al-Sereiti 1999 (PMID 10641130). Caffeic-acid/dihydroxyphenyllactic acid metabolites circulate. Significant plasma levels achievable from spearmint tea at high-RMA cultivar (Connelly 2014, in Luo 2020 review PMC7059186) — 16 weeks improved knee OA stiffness/disability/pain.
compounds.regulatory_status: Rosemary extract is FDA GRAS (21 CFR 182.10, 182.20). Spearmint and lemon balm are FDA GRAS. Rosmarinic acid as isolated compound is dietary supplement / cosmetic ingredient, not FDA drug.
compounds.human_use_precedent: Spearmint tea standardized to high-RMA cultivar (Connelly 2014) — 16 weeks RCT, OA pain. Multiple botanical supplements (rosemary leaf, lemon balm) as dietary supplements at gram-scale.

platform_relevance.verdict: DIETARY-VIABLE upstream-CP0 modulator. The single best-anchored natural-product C3-convertase inhibitor in the literature. Multiple in vivo precedents (rat CVF lung injury, mouse + rat ADPKD) at single-digit mg/kg.
platform_relevance.gout_specific_logic: C3 convertase is the dominant initiator of MSU-driven complement activation per complement-c5a-gout.md §2.6 (classical pathway dominant; alternative pathway amplifies). Blocking C3 convertase upstream prevents both C3a and C5a generation. This is the chokepoint-hacker move comp-018 was designed to find.
open_questions: Does oral RMA (vs i.v. in Proctor 2006) achieve enough lumen-side concentration to dampen mucosal-macrophage CP0 priming during a flare? RMA is water-soluble; gut-luminal concentration after gram-scale rosemary supplementation is plausibly mid-µM range, within the in vitro IC50 window.
Is the C3-convertase inhibition surface-targeted (where MSU lives) or fluid-phase-only? The 1988 paper suggests both.

compounds.name: Luteolin
compounds.chembl_anticomplement_data: EMPTY for anticomplement; XO IC50 550 nM (J Nat Prod 1998); ABCG2 IC50 8.9 µM (J Nat Prod 2011); CYP1B1 IC50 79 nM
compounds.target_node: C3 convertase (classical AND alternative pathway — dual)

ic50_data.primary_source: Zhang T, Chen D, J Ethnopharmacol 2008;117(2):351-61 (PMID 18400428, doi:10.1016/j.jep.2008.02.012)
ic50_data.verification_status: abstract-anchored — paper reports CH50 = 0.19 mM = 190 µM
abstract-anchored — AP50 = 0.17 mM = 170 µM. Most-potent flavonoid in the 9-flavonoid panel screened.

compounds.bioavailability_oral: ~5% systemic (heavy glucuronidation; Shimoi 1998 PMID 9625363). Plasma luteolin glucuronide is the main circulating form. Dietary intake ~1 mg/day; supplement dosing 100-300 mg.
compounds.regulatory_status: Dietary flavonoid; supplement-tier; FDA-recognized as generally consumed in vegetables.
compounds.human_use_precedent: Standard dietary flavonoid; commercial supplements 100-300 mg/day.

platform_relevance.verdict: DUAL-MODE candidate. ALREADY IN OE corpus (comp-013 GUT-LUMINAL VIABLE for XO + URAT1). comp-018 adds upstream-complement modulation as a third mechanism. The CH50/AP50 IC50 in mid-100 µM range is achievable in gut lumen at 100-300 mg supplement dosing (gut concentration ~mM at gram-scale). Systemic concentration too low for systemic anticomplement effect.
platform_relevance.gout_specific_logic: Luteolin is the only compound in OE corpus to date with documented activity at THREE gout chokepoints simultaneously: XO (550 nM, biochem), URAT1 (animal-model expression downregulation, comp-013), and now upstream-complement C3 convertase (CP+AP at ~190 µM). Luminal-side activity profile is internally consistent across all three.
open_questions: The 190 µM IC50 is achievable in gut lumen but the systemic glucuronide form has unknown anticomplement activity — the conjugate may retain or lose activity vs aglycone.
Synergy with rosmarinic acid (different mechanism — convertase inhibition vs flavonoid-mediated C3-binding interference) is plausible but untested.

compounds.name: Tiliroside (kaempferol 3-O-beta-D-(6"-O-coumaroyl)glucopyranoside)
compounds.target_node: Classical pathway (likely C3 convertase based on Englberger comparator)

ic50_data.primary_source: Jung KY et al., Biol Pharm Bull 1998;21(10):1077-8 (PMID 9821813, doi:10.1248/bpb.21.1077)
ic50_data.verification_status: abstract-anchored — IC50 = 5.4 × 10⁻⁵ M = 54 µM. Reported as ~3-4× more potent than rosmarinic acid (in same-assay comparator, Jung lab convention)

compounds.bioavailability_oral: Glycoside likely deglycosylated by gut microbiota; aglycone (kaempferol) BA ~2%; significant gut microbiome metabolism. Limited PK data.
compounds.human_use_precedent: Magnolia bark (Hou Po) is TCM materia medica; tiliroside content variable

platform_relevance.verdict: MECHANISTICALLY CLEAN candidate. Cleaner than rosmarinic acid in vitro by some assay formats. No in vivo anticomplement evidence.

compounds.name: Falcarindiol ((3S,8S)-falcarindiol)

ic50_data.primary_source: Chung IM et al., Phytother Res 2011;25(5):784-6 (PMID 21520473, doi:10.1002/ptr.3336)

compounds.bioavailability_oral: Lipophilic; carrots and celery contain at low ppm levels; supplement-tier dosing not established

platform_relevance.verdict: MOST-POTENT in vitro classical-pathway natural-product inhibitor identified so far at IC50 15.2 µM. Bioavailability and dietary scale-up are unsolved problems.

compounds.name: Gallocatechin-(4'-O-7)-epigallocatechin biflavanol
natural_sources: Bridelia ferruginea (stem bark, West African medicinal)

ic50_data.verification_status: abstract-tier — paper reports <10 µM, more potent than rosmarinic acid in same assay

compounds.regulatory_status: Not regulated as a supplement in US/EU; ethnomedicinal use in West Africa

platform_relevance.verdict: BENCH CURIOSITY — most-potent in vitro hit but no human use precedent at scale outside ethnomedicine, no commercial supplement, no PK

DIETARY-VIABLE — dicaffeoylquinic acids are abundant in artichoke leaf, honeysuckle, and chicory. Sub-10 µM IC50 against C1 component. Worth a placeholder slot in any future supplement-stack consideration.
LIMITED SOURCE — Bridelia ferruginea-specific; not in commercial dietary stream

compounds.name: Ergosterol
compounds.bioavailability_oral: Sterol — converted to vitamin D2 with UV exposure; intestinal absorption ~30% at supplement doses; lipophilic
compounds.regulatory_status: Dietary supplement (vitamin D2 precursor); FDA recognized
compounds.human_use_precedent: Mushroom consumption at ~1-3 g dry/day common; reishi extract supplements 1-9 g/day

platform_relevance.verdict: DIETARY-VIABLE if reishi-supplement context matters. comp-014 already documents reishi (Ganoderma lucidum) as an anchor species; comp-018 adds ergosterol as a CP-modulating constituent. Triple convergence with comp-014's β-glucan caveat (structure-dependent NLRP3 effect) — practical messaging needs care.

ic50_data.verification_status: abstract-anchored — most-potent triterpene in 8-compound Ganoderma panel

platform_relevance.verdict: FUNGAL-CHEMISTRY-DISTINCTIVE — adds anticomplement to Ganoderma's pharmacology stack; most potent of 8 ganoderma triterpenes screened. Internal-cassette engineering candidate (fermentable in Ganoderma cultivation pipelines). SAR insight: Δ7(8), Δ9(11)-lanostadiene + 3-keto required.
WEAK — included for fungal-coverage completeness
STRUCTURAL SAR DATA — α-OH at C-3 + COOH at C-23 positions required for activity

ic50_data.primary_source: Mu LH et al., Fitoterapia 2013;92:41-5 (PMID 24144800, doi:10.1016/j.fitote.2013.10.005)
ic50_data.comparator_note: RMA reported as 181.8 µM in same-assay — discrepancy with Englberger 1988 (5-10 µM). Same-paper comparator suggests downstream literature uses a less-potent assay endpoint.

platform_relevance.verdict: WEAK in absolute terms; novel scaffold class

ic50_data.primary_source: Moon HI et al., Immunopharmacol Immunotoxicol 2012;34(2):299-302 (PMID 21854169, doi:10.3109/08923973.2011.602690)
ic50_data.retraction_warning: PAPER RETRACTED — see PubMed record. Sorgoleone series claim must be considered unverified.

platform_relevance.verdict: RETRACTED — drop from candidate list except as cautionary note

ic50_data.primary_source: Jung S et al., Immunopharmacol Immunotoxicol 2012;34(2):244-6 (PMID 21854097)
ic50_data.retraction_warning: PAPER RETRACTED — drop activity claim from this paper.

ic50_data.assay: Same compound surfaced again in Zhang & Chen 2008 (PMID 18400428) classical/alternative pathway flavonoid panel as inactive vs CH50/AP50 at panel concentrations

platform_relevance.verdict: MIXED EVIDENCE — primary positive paper retracted; secondary panel shows weak activity

compounds.name: Quercetin
compounds.chembl_anticomplement_data: EMPTY for anticomplement; broad ChEMBL coverage for other targets

ic50_data.primary_source: Zhang T, Chen D, J Ethnopharmacol 2008 (PMID 18400428)
ic50_data.verification_status: abstract-tier — quercetin (#11 in 15-compound panel) shown active in CP+AP, less potent than luteolin
ic50_data.qualitative: ACTIVE in panel; specific IC50 not extracted from abstract

ic50_data.assay: Quercetin via gut microbiota deglycosylation of rutin
ic50_data.primary_source: Jin X et al., J Pharm Biomed Anal 2020;184:113176 (PMID 32092632, doi:10.1016/j.jpba.2020.113176)
ic50_data.qualitative: Quercetin (and formononetin, maackiain) anticomplement activity ENHANCED relative to glycoside parents after gut bacteria deglycosylation. Implication: oral rutin/quercetin glycoside supplements likely deliver active aglycone via microbiome conversion in the colon.

compounds.bioavailability_oral: Aglycone BA ~2-5%; glycosides (rutin) ~20%; significant microbiome conversion

platform_relevance.verdict: DIETARY-VIABLE; gut-microbiome-enhanced anticomplement makes it more relevant for gut-luminal CP0 modulation than systemic. Already in OE supplements adjacency.

ic50_data.qualitative: ACTIVE in 9-flavonoid panel (Zhang & Chen 2008); less potent than luteolin

platform_relevance.verdict: DIETARY; weaker than luteolin; supplement-tier
Chrysanthemum-derived; classical-TCM marker compound
Glycoside parent — likely deglycosylated to quercetin in gut

compounds.name: Bupleurum smithii var. parvifolium polysaccharides (BPs)
compounds.molecular_weight_g_mol: polysaccharide MW range, not single compound

ic50_data.verification_status: anchored via Paperclip cat of PMC4629277

ic50_data.assay: Lectin pathway IC50 — Bupleurum chinense polysaccharides

compounds.bioavailability_oral: Polysaccharide — minimal systemic absorption; gut-luminal effect dominant
compounds.regulatory_status: Bupleurum (Chai Hu) is TCM materia medica; classical use for fever/inflammation

platform_relevance.verdict: GUT-LUMINAL VIABLE — high-MW polysaccharide stays in gut lumen at gram-scale Chai Hu decoctions; lectin-pathway inhibition is mechanistically distinct from convertase inhibition; complementary to RMA and luteolin. Supports the CNKI-language gap thesis (Chinese pharmaceutical literature has substantial Bupleurum anticomplement work that ChEMBL has not curated).

ic50_data.qualitative: Anticomplement activity referenced as known phenotype; PD1-1 specifically characterized for antioxidant + α-amylase/α-glucosidase inhibition (IC50 0.53 / 0.40 mg/mL)
ic50_data.primary_source: Guo H et al., Molecules 2019;24(8):1485 (PMID 30991766, doi:10.3390/molecules24081485)

platform_relevance.verdict: DIETARY/HERBAL — dandelion is FDA GRAS; Korean-Japanese pharmaceutical interest in dandelion polysaccharide anticomplement is documented but not deeply quantified in English literature. Worth direct CNKI/J-STAGE search.

compounds.name: Complestatin
natural_sources: Streptomyces species (and S. xinghaiensis NRRL B-24674 with related BGC)
compounds.target_node: C5 inhibition (positions complestatin in same general class as K-76)

ic50_data.note: anti-HIV activity at sub-µM; original positioning is 'an anti-complement agent'; antiviral activity is downstream

ic50_data.qualitative: Streptomyces xinghaiensis NRRL B-24674 culture broth shows good anticomplement activity; nrps1 BGC disruption abolishes activity, confirming this is the responsible BGC class
ic50_data.primary_source: Chen LY et al., Appl Microbiol Biotechnol 2018;102(22):9549-9562 (PMID 30232534, doi:10.1007/s00253-018-9337-2)

platform_relevance.verdict: ENGINEERING-RELEVANT — first identified bacterial-NRPS-derived natural-product anticomplement agent. The biosynthetic gene cluster is heterologously expressible (Streptomyces engineering precedent). Not a candidate for direct dietary use, but the fact that complement-modulator chemistry sits in cultivable bacterial BGC space is platform-relevant: an engineered LBP chassis (engineered-lbp-chassis.md) carrying complestatin-related BGC could be a novel CP0 modality. Unlikely tractable in koji (NRPS clusters are bacterial), but ties into the LBP peer track.

compounds.name: K-76 (filifolinol-derived sesquiterpene) and K-76-COOH
compounds.target_node: C5 inhibitor — blocks downstream of C5 convertase

ic50_data.primary_source: Larghi EL et al., Eur J Med Chem 2012;55:74-84 (PMID 22835722, doi:10.1016/j.ejmech.2012.07.003)
ic50_data.verification_status: abstract-anchored — K-76 COOH IC50 = 570 µM. 7-trifluoromethyl semi-synthetic analog improved to 100 µM.

ic50_data.assay: in vivo C5 inhibition in murine LPS shock
ic50_data.qualitative: K-76 COOH protected MDP-primed C3H/HeJ mice from LPS-induced anaphylactoid shock; inhibited platelet degradation

platform_relevance.verdict: FUNGAL-CHEMISTRY-DISTINCTIVE C5 inhibitor — same upstream-of-C5a logic that comp-018 is hunting for. Native fungal producer (Stachybotrys is mycotoxic — exclude per toxicity-filter), but the BGC is candidate for engineered-koji heterologous expression in a nontoxigenic chassis. Synthetic derivatives improve potency 5×. Open question: can koji express the K-76 sesquiterpene synthase + downstream functionalization enzymes?

compounds.name: Suramin
compounds.target_node: Lectin pathway AND classical pathway (broad polyanion)

ic50_data.qualitative: Suramin used as positive control in Bupleurum lectin-pathway study (Wu 2015); confirmed to block lectin and classical pathways at standard pharmacological concentrations

compounds.regulatory_status: FDA-approved for African trypanosomiasis (orphan); off-label oncology trials; NOT a dietary supplement

platform_relevance.verdict: FDA-approved drug repurposing surface for upstream-CP0; mechanism is non-specific polyanion. Long history of complement-research use as positive control. Not OE-platform-applicable directly but documents the FDA-approved repurposing surface exists.

compounds.name: Heparin / Reviparin (low-molecular-weight heparin)
natural_sources: mammalian intestinal mucosa / lung (animal-source); modern recombinant
compounds.target_node: Alternative pathway (C3bBb assembly) — Factor H mimetic

ic50_data.assay: Reviparin attenuated complement-mediated myocardial injury in isolated rabbit heart
ic50_data.qualitative: Heparin's anticomplement effect operates via Factor H-mimetic surface binding — reduced fluid-phase Bb formation

compounds.regulatory_status: FDA-approved anticoagulant; not an OE-platform candidate; LMWH dietary use NOT applicable

platform_relevance.verdict: MECHANISTIC INSIGHT — heparin/sulfated polysaccharides bind C3b and recruit Factor H, accelerating decay. This is the same mechanism endogenous DAF/CD55 + Factor H use. Implication: any heparin-mimetic sulfated polysaccharide (chondroitin sulfate, dermatan sulfate, fucoidans from brown algae) is a candidate complement modulator via this mechanism. Brown-algae fucoidans deserve a sub-search.

compounds.name: C1-INH (purified human plasma protein)

ic50_data.qualitative: C1-INH at physiologic concentrations (~250 µg/mL plasma) inhibits AP via direct C3b binding + Factor B competition; replacement therapy used in HAE

compounds.regulatory_status: FDA-approved (Berinert, Cinryze) for hereditary angioedema (HAE) — not gout-applicable directly but biochemical anchor

platform_relevance.verdict: ENGINEERING ANCHOR — C1-INH is a soluble human serpin; recombinant production is established (Ruconest = recombinant human C1-INH from rabbit milk). Could a koji or LBP chassis express recombinant C1-INH at gut-luminal scale? This is the same engineering thesis as the DAF SCR1-4 truncation (H05) — different soluble human complement regulator, same chassis logic. Phase 2 follow-up: open a parallel comp-NNN for koji-expressed C1-INH.

compounds.name: Phenylethanoid glycosides — isoilicifolioside A, ilicifolioside A, campneoside II, isocampneoside II

ic50_data.comparator: tiliroside IC50 104 µM; rosmarinic acid IC50 182 µM in same assay (assay-format note: this is the high-µM RMA range, not Englberger 1988 5-10 µM)

platform_relevance.verdict: Paulownia wood is not in food chain; ethnomedicinal only

ic50_data.primary_source: Austarheim I et al., J Ethnopharmacol 2016;190:159-64 (PMID 27260410, doi:10.1016/j.jep.2016.05.058)

platform_relevance.verdict: Ethnomedicinal Mali use for cerebral malaria; not in food chain

compounds.name: Avacopan
compounds.target_node: C5aR1 (DOWNSTREAM, not upstream — included for OE corpus continuity)

ic50_data.qualitative: FDA-approved 2021 (Tavneos) for ANCA-associated vasculitis. Already in OE corpus per complement-c5a-gout.md. NOT comp-018 candidate — C5aR1 is downstream of the upstream nodes comp-018 targets. Documented here only for completeness.

platform_relevance.verdict: Already documented in OE corpus; not natural-product; downstream not upstream

ic50_data.qualitative: FDA-approved for PNH, aHUS, gMG, NMO. Mechanism = direct C5 binding, blocks cleavage to C5a. Already in OE corpus.

platform_relevance.verdict: Pharma anchor for upstream-of-C5a class; demonstrates that the C5 cleavage step IS druggable; biologic, not OE-platform-applicable

compounds.name: Pegcetacoplan
natural_sources: synthetic peptide derived from compstatin (Lambris lab) which itself derived from C3-binding phage display
compounds.target_node: C3 (upstream — blocks all three pathways at C3)

ic50_data.qualitative: FDA-approved 2021 (Empaveli) for PNH. Mechanism = direct C3 binding, blocks all pathway convergence at C3.

platform_relevance.verdict: Synthetic peptide, not natural product. Documents that C3 binding is a viable upstream-CP0 chokepoint pharmacologically. The compstatin scaffold (C3-binding cyclic peptide ~13 aa) is potentially heterologously expressible in koji or LBP — a Phase 2 open question.

ic50_data.qualitative: FDA-approved 2023 (Fabhalta) for PNH, IgA nephropathy, C3 glomerulopathy. Mechanism = Factor B binding, blocks AP convertase formation.

platform_relevance.verdict: Pharma anchor for Factor B as druggable upstream-CP0 node. Demonstrates that even modest oral small-molecule potency at Factor B can dampen complement clinically. The natural-product literature has zero direct Factor B antagonists — gap.

ic50_data.qualitative: FDA-approved 2024 (Voydeya) as add-on PNH therapy. Mechanism = Factor D serine protease inhibition.

platform_relevance.verdict: Pharma anchor for Factor D. Natural-product Factor D inhibitors: zero direct hits in this sweep.

ic50_data.qualitative: FDA-approved 2025 (Omeros / Omsipro) for HSCT-TMA. Mechanism = MASP-2 binding.

platform_relevance.verdict: Pharma anchor for MASP-2. Lectin pathway is non-dominant in MSU but ingestion-relevant. Bupleurum polysaccharides (above) are the natural-product analog at this node.

compounds_searched_no_evidence: EGCG / epigallocatechin gallate — direct PubMed search ("complement inhibitor" AND EGCG) returned zero results despite EGCG being broadly anti-inflammatory. No documented direct anticomplement IC50 found.

Curcumin — does not directly inhibit complement at upstream nodes; one study (PMID 23410788) shows curcumin at 30 mg/kg in mouse SLE model AGGRAVATED CNS lupus by reducing brain C3 deposits; another shows complex effects on apolipoprotein C-III. NOT a clean upstream-CP0 modulator.
Resveratrol — protects RPE against AP-induced cell death (PMID 23518773) but mechanism is antioxidant, not direct complement inhibition.
Berberine — broad immunomodulatory effects in fish complement (PMID 27235371); no direct human anticomplement IC50.
Baicalein — searched; no direct anticomplement hit at upstream nodes
Magnolol / Honokiol — searched; large complement literature but no clear direct upstream node IC50
Glycyrrhizin / Astragalus polysaccharide — broad immunomodulatory; specific upstream-node IC50 not surfaced in this sweep
Cordycepin — specific anticomplement evidence not in this sweep (cordycepin's gout-relevant evidence is at URAT1/XO/ADA, not complement)
Polysaccharide-K (PSK) / Trametes versicolor — anti-tumor / immunomodulator; specific anticomplement IC50 not in this sweep
Lentinan — Lentinula edodes β-glucan; immunostimulant (NLRP3-adjacent); specific anticomplement IC50 not in this sweep
Beta-glucans generally — structure-dependent NLRP3 modulation per comp-014 Phase 5; complement effects per beta-glucan structure mixed (β-1,3 activate via lectin pathway in some assays)
Short-chain fatty acids (butyrate, propionate) — substantial inflammation-modulatory literature but no direct upstream-complement IC50 surfaced in PubMed for anticomplement
Direct C5aR1 antagonists in fungi/plants — confirmed empty per comp-014 and §1.21 (re-confirmed via this sweep)
translation_disagreement_summary: No translation-disagreement annotations were triggered in this Phase 1 sweep. The corpus surfaced was English-language PubMed-indexed primary literature plus PMC full-text where available. Phase 2 (planned) would do direct CNKI/WanFang/J-STAGE searches for Bupleurum, Ganoderma, dandelion, Sophora tonkinensis, and Magnolia tiliroside literature — those are the most-likely-to-have-non-English-evidence compound classes. Per CLAUDE.md, Phase 2 translation cross-check would use Claude + DeepSeek V4-Pro for Chinese sources.
